## [LSU Master's Theses](https://repository.lsu.edu/gradschool_theses)

# [Crop yield predictions through satellite remote sensing and machine learning: Case studies in cotton and sugarcane](https://repository.lsu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=7146&context=gradschool_theses)

## Author

[**Dulis Duron Chevez**, *Louisiana State University and Agricultural and Mechanical College*](https://repository.lsu.edu/do/search/?q=%28author%3A%22Dulis%20Duron%20Chevez%22%20AND%20-bp_author_id%3A%5B%2A%20TO%20%2A%5D%29%20OR%20bp_author_id%3A%28%224d04838d-3316-4e54-b6a1-87944ce349f6%22%29&start=0&context=8403704)[Follow](https://network.bepress.com/api/follow/subscribe?user=NThlYjU1ZDNjNjUyNGY0ZQ%3D%3D&institution=NDJjNWFkNzRjOGRlZGY0Nw%3D%3D&format=html "Follow Dulis Duron Chevez")

## Semester of Graduation

Fall 2024

## Degree

Master of Science (MS)

## Department

School of Plant, Environmental and Soil Sciences

## Document Type

Thesis

## Abstract

Traditional methods for crop yield prediction, such as manual stalk counting in sugarcane and boll counting in cotton, are labor-intensive and not feasible for large-scale field estimates. This research explores using satellite remote sensing and machine learning (ML) techniques to predict crop yield for cotton and sugarcane. Six machine learning models were used: Random Forest (RF), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Partial Least Squares Regression (PLSR), Xtreme Gradient Boosting (XGB) and Simple linear regression (SLR). Among these models, RF consistently showed the best performance for predicting yield for both crops, showing lower RMSEs. The integration of vegetation indices (VIs) such as NDVI, GNDVI, and NDRE, and multi-temporal satellite data significantly improved model accuracy. Moreover, yield prediction accuracy increased as crops matured further into the growing season, with the RF model performing better in later growth stages showing RMSE values below 4200 kg ha⁻¹ and 259 kg ha⁻¹ for cane yield and cotton yield, respectively. The results prove the effectiveness of combining remote sensing and ML as a field-scale precision agriculture technique for yield prediction in both cotton and sugarcane. This study highlights the potential of advanced data analytics for improving resource management and decision-making for sugarcane and cotton producers.

## Date

10-30-2024

## Recommended Citation

Duron Chevez, Dulis, "Crop yield predictions through satellite remote sensing and machine learning: Case studies in cotton and sugarcane" (2024). *LSU Master's Theses*. 6054.
  
https://repository.lsu.edu/gradschool\_theses/6054

--- SOURCE 5: Improving Crop Yield Predictions with Satellite Assist ---
URL: https://landsat.gsfc.nasa.gov/article/improving-crop-yield-predictions-with-satellite-assist/

SUMMARY:
Improving Crop Yield Predictions with Satellite Assist | Landsat Science Michigan State University’s Earth and Environmental Studies & Plant, Soil, and Microbial Sciences departments are conducting research aimed at creating better crop yield predictions. New Michigan State University research found that incorporating in-season water deficit information into remote sensing-based crop models significantly improves corn yield predictions. With the ultimate goal of using digital technology, specifically a machine learning model based on remote-sensed imagery and data from a cumulative drop index, to help farmers make more informed decisions, Basso and Shuai began their research by studying 352 fields of varying sizes in Michigan, Indiana, Illinois and Iowa, collecting climate, soil data and more than 2,500 yield maps.

DESCRIPTION: A diagram illustrating a system for predicting rice crop extent and yield using UAV imagery data, featuring various components like feature extractors (Alexnet and CNN_2conv), weather data input, and visual representations of yield predictions.

DESCRIPTION: None

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://pub.mdpi-res.com/remotesensing/remotesensing-16-01003/article_deploy/html/images/remotesensing-16-01003-ag.png?1710299485
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates the use of machine learning and deep learning techniques for crop yield estimation, highlighting various image types, such as multi-spectral and hyperspectral images, and tools like drones and cameras that aid in guiding agricultural production.

DESCRIPTION: None

enriched_points.expanded_content: Remote sensing enhances yield forecasting accuracy by integrating satellite data with crop models, enabling real-time insights into growth conditions and predicting harvest outcomes effectively.

Utilizing remote sensing technologies allows for comprehensive monitoring of crop health by analyzing vegetation indices, identifying stress factors, and supporting timely interventions to optimize growth.
Remote sensing provides critical insights on resource allocation, helping farmers optimize water usage and fertilizer applications by monitoring environmental factors and crop needs in real-time.
